coredata.dc:title: Development of aptasensor for chlorpyrifos detection using paper-based screen-printed electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Himanshu, Jayendra Kumar
Lakshmi, G.B.V.S.
Verma, Awadhesh Kumar
Ahlawat, Amit
Solanki, Pratima R.
coredata.dc:description: Novel Carbon quantum dots-graphite composite ink-based Screen-printed electrodes (CQDs/SPEs) were used to assemble a highly sensitive electrochemical aptasensor against chlorpyrifos (CPF). The aptasensor showed a broad linear range from 1 pM (0.445 ng/ml) to 500 nM (0.22 mg/ml) with a detection limit (LOD) 0.834 pM (0.37 ng/ml); sensitivity 21.39 μA pM−1 cm− 2 and with good linearity of R2 = 0.973. Moreover, the aptasensor's showed better selectivity among few other pesticides. Further, the aptasensor electrode showed high stability for five months when stored at 4 °C. In the final step, the aptasensor's ability to identify CPF in real samples was evaluated on spiked potato (Solanum tuberosum) extract samples. Potato extract spiked with CPF in the electrochemical aptasensing platform showed excellent linearity of R2 = 0.981. The developed aptasensor showed good response to without spiked potato extract with increasing volumes. Hence, the developed aptasensor demonstrated reasonable applicability in real food and agriculture samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Screen-printed electrode
Aptasensor
Chlorpyrifos
Electrochemical
Potato

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Screen printing electrode for the fabrication of aptasensor
Electrochemical response study and interference study of aptasensor CQDs/SPE electrodes
Reproducibility, stability, spiked studies of potato extract samples against aptasensing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Advanced Instrument Research Facility (AIRF), Jawaharlal Nehru University (JNU), New Delhi, for providing a TEM characterization facility. GBVSL is thankful to DST for funding through DST Women Scientist Project. The authors are also grateful to the Indian Council of medical research (ICMR), India (PAC–SCNS–PS-ICMR-1293) for providing financial support and motivation.

The authors are also grateful to the Indian Council of medical research ( ICMR ), India ( PAC–SCNS–PS-ICMR-1293 ) for providing financial support and motivation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectrum (b) Zeta potential study (c)TEM image (scale: 5 nm) and corresponding (d) Size distribution of CQDs.
Cyclic voltammetry (A) and chronoamperometry (B) curves of bare and aptamer electrodes and (C) FTIR spectra of (a) CQDs (b) Aptamer (c) CQDs with aptamer (inset: zoomed view 1500-1000 cm−1).
Electrokinetic analysis of CQDs/SPE electrodes (insets: linear plot of the square root of scan rate versus peak current, top left; the square root of scan rate versus peak voltage, down right).
(a) Electrochemical response studies for the detection of CPF and (b) Interferant study of aptasensor electrodes against several interferants.

(a) Reproducibility of SPE electrodes of aptasensor. (b) Stability of CQDs/SPE electrodes over time for the aptasensor until five months. (c) Response study of CQDs/SPE electrodes for different conc. of CPF in the spiked potato sample. (d) Current response with Potato extract volume against aptasensing platform.
The fabrication and working mechanism of the CQDs/SPEs aptasensor for CPF detection.
Potato extract analysis done against CQDs/SPEs aptasensor platform.
Our aptasensing platform is compared to previously published literature.

row.entry: Fluorine doped tin-oxide (FTO) with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)
Human serum albumin (HSA) and the Pd-doped CdTe quantum dots (Pd:CdTe)

head.ce:title: Development of aptasensor for chlorpyrifos detection using paper-based screen-printed electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Special Centre for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University (JNU), New Delhi, 110067, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Special Centre for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University (JNU), New Delhi-110067, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, School of Life Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi Central University, Motihari, Bihar, 845401, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, School of Life Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi Central University, Motihari, Bihar-845401, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology, Murthal-131039

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel Carbon quantum dots-graphite composite ink-based Screen-printed electrodes (CQDs/SPEs) were used to assemble a highly sensitive electrochemical aptasensor against chlorpyrifos (CPF). The aptasensor showed a broad linear range from 1 pM (0.445 ng/ml) to 500 nM (0.22 mg/ml) with a detection limit (LOD) 0.834 pM (0.37 ng/ml); sensitivity 21.39 μA pM−1 cm− 2 and with good linearity of R2 = 0.973. Moreover, the aptasensor's showed better selectivity among few other pesticides. Further, the aptasensor electrode showed high stability for five months when stored at 4 °C. In the final step, the aptasensor's ability to identify CPF in real samples was evaluated on spiked potato (Solanum tuberosum) extract samples. Potato extract spiked with CPF in the electrochemical aptasensing platform showed excellent linearity of R2 = 0.981. The developed aptasensor showed good response to without spiked potato extract with increasing volumes. Hence, the developed aptasensor demonstrated reasonable applicability in real food and agriculture samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus group pesticides are commonly used in crop production to protect plants against pests and diseases although they pose food safety issues (D. J. Fu et al., 2019). The environmental contamination of food chains and their proximity to dangerous agrochemical products are the main causes of adulteration and toxicity. Pesticides are among the many toxins, such as fertilizers, antibiotics, and pesticides, that have been linked to serious health issues in people. Among, organophosphates are vastly used in agriculture due to their broad-spectrum effects. But the toxicity of organophosphate pesticides has been a significant source of concern worldwide because of its devastating effects on the nervous and reproductive systems of living organisms (Bala et al., 2016). Chlorpyrifos (CPF), an organophosphate group pesticide, is extensively used in the international market (Feng et al., 2017). It can effectively hold acetylcholinesterase, blocking signal transmission between neurons, as a result, the pest's central nervous system collapse and ultimately causes death (Feng et al., 2017).

CPF detection is usually done by advanced and sophisticated instrumentation such as Gas chromatography (GC), high-pressure liquid chromatography (HPLC), and mass spectrometry (MS) (Cacho et al., 2018; Chang et al., 2019; Gaweł et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019) which are time-consuming, require expensive instrumentation, involve complex testing procedure and trained personnel (Uniyal and Sharma, 2018; Yang et al., 2021). Hence, there is a need to develop a fast detection method for CPF because the detection plays a key role in ensuring food safety. Electrochemical approach has gained popularity due to its speedy and extremely sensitive reaction as well as its simplicity in integrating with a variety of biorecognition components like aptamers, enzymes, or antibodies (J. Fu et al., 2019). In electrochemical sensors, aptamers offer excellent selectivity and sensitivity with readily observable signals, in comparison to other biorecognition components (Vanani et al., 2021). Aptamers are artificial single-stranded RNA or DNA molecules with a particular 3D structure (Sefah et al., 2010). However, the published aptasensors for CPF are quite limited. Also, as compared to other biomolecules such as enzymes, antibodies, aptamers have different advantages such as very selective, very stable at room temperature, preservation, modification, and exceptional thermal stability (Qiao et al., 2015). Aptamers can be used in the development of very sensitive and selective biosensors. The sensitivity of electrochemical sensors can be further improved by incorporating the nanomaterials in the sensing platform.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) offer a number of desirable qualities from a nanotechnology and material science standpoint because they are used in widespread applications, including aptasensors (Gao et al., 2004; Lim et al., 2015). CQDs show good chemical stability, susceptibility to chemical modifications, good relative conductivity, biocompatibility, cost-effectiveness, and easy synthesis process. CQDs facilitate enhanced immobilization properties of biological molecules such as antibodies, aptamer or DNA (Qu et al., 2013; Sri et al., 2021). Aptamer-based biosensors excelled in several applications including biosensors when compared to antibodies and enzymes. Talan et al. reported immuno-sensing platform for detection of CPF within the range from 1 fM to 1 μM and LOD 10 fM using fluorine doped tin-oxide (FTO) glass coated with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) (Talan et al., 2018). Hunde et al. reported electrochemical sensor for detection of CPF with linear range from 1.0 to 100 μM and the LOD 2.8 x10−6 mol/L using Nano Fe3O4–Graphite Paste Modified Cu electrode (Hunde et al., 2017). In other report, Wang et al. reported photoelectrochemical sensor for detection of CPF with linear range from 0.01 to 80 ng/mL and the detection limit 0.03 ng/mL using β-Bi2O3/g-C3N4 heterojunction (Wang et al., 2021). A photoelectrochemical biosensor for detection of CPF in the linear range: 0.001–1.5 μg/mL and the LOD 0.07 ng/mL using nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (Cheng et al., 2019) was reported. Chandra et al. reported fluorometric sensor for CPF detection in the linear range of 0.02–0.18 μg/mL and the detection limits 2.7 ng/mL using fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized from Jatropha fruits (Chandra et al., 2022). A molecular imprinted voltametric sensor for detection of chlorpyrifos with linear range from 1.0 × 10−11–1.0 × 10−9 M and the detection limits 2.0 × 10−12 M using Carbon Nitride Nanotubes Decorated with Graphene Quantum Dots (Yola and Atar, 2017) was reported by Yola et al. The present work reports an electrochemical aptasensor developed on screen printed electrode (SPE), in which the carbon ink was modified with CQDs, hence different from other reported works and enhance the novelty of presented work.

In present study, an innovative electrochemical aptasensor for CPF detection was fabricated on the CQDs/SPEs sensor platform. The CQDs established an effective biomolecules immobilization platform on paper-based substrate with high binding affinity towards aptamers against CPF pesticide. This research article implicates a novel way to fabricate aptasensor for chlorpyrifos with broader linear response range and a reduced LOD under optimal conditions, indicating that this sensor has been demonstrated to be an effective approach for the detection of CPF. Further, this sensor is found to be more sensitive as compared to the LCMS in terms of detection of CPF in real samples at lower concentrations.

Monosodium phosphate (NaH2PO4) and Disodium phosphate hydrate (Na2HPO4) were procured from Sisco Research Laboratory, India. Potassium ferricyanide III (K3[Fe(CN)6]), potassium ferricyanide II trihydrate (K4[Fe(CN)6].3H2O) and potassium chloride (KCl) were procured from fisher scientific. Chlorpyrifos (CPF), carbofuran, methyl parathion (MP) Aldicarb (ALD) propoxur and monocrotophos (MC) were purchased from Sigma. Aptamer against CPF was produced at Creative Biogene in the United States of America and purchased through Genetree eBiolab in Delhi. In the laboratory, Tris buffer and PBS (phosphate buffer solution) were prepared. PURELAB pulse water purification equipment was used to obtain distilled (DI) water. The chemical compounds of analytical grade are used in this work.

For electrochemical studies, PBS 0.1 M comprising 5 mM ferri/ferrocyanide [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- at pH 7.4 was used. For dilutions of aptamer and CPF, Tris buffer solution was prepared using 10 mM Tris HCl, 0.1 mM EDTA with adjusted pH 7.5. It was used to dilute aptamer against CPF to 5 μM. Using serial dilution, various dilutions of CPF from 1 pM–500 nM (1, 10, 100 pM, 1, 5, 10, 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 nM) were prepared in Tris buffer of pH of 7.5.

The interference studies were carried out on different pesticide as well as various common chemical compounds present in fruits and vegetables. The pesticide and metal compounds used for interference studied are as follows; glucose (Glu) (4 mM), K, Zn, Mg, Ca, Na, Mn each of 1 mg/ml, further pesticides such as Carbofuran (20 nM), monocrotophos (MC) (1 nM), Aldicarb (ALD) (20 nM), Propoxur (10 nM) and methyl parathion (MP) (25 nM). In the presence of CPF (5 nM), they all were added one by one.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used in ATR mode (PerkinElmer, US) to study functional groups present in fabricated aptasensor electrodes. The morphological structure of microwave synthesized CQDs was studied on Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOLJEM-2200 FS, Japan). Zeta analyzer (ZEECOM, Microtec Co., LTD., Japan) was used to determine the overall charge on the surface of CQDs. The electrochemical studies of fabricated aptasensor electrodes were carried out by chronoamperometry techniques using Ametek PARSTAT MC Potentiostat and CV using Autolab Galvanostat/Potentiostat (EcoChemie, The, Netherlands). In CV, applied potential was from −0.8 – +0.8 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s.

The CQDs were formulated by a modified microwave synthesis method using citric acid and polyethyleneimine (PEI) as precursors as given in reference (Ahlawat et al., 2021). Briefly, 1 g citric acid in 10 ml of DI and stir for 5 min at 250 rpm in a beaker. A few drops of PEI were added to the solution of citric acid, the solution was kept in the microwave for 3 min at 720 W. The beaker was rinsed with 10 ml of DI water and obtained the CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screen printing electrode for the fabrication of aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The screen-printed electrodes (CQDs/SPEs) were printed on paper-based substrate with indigenously prepared graphite ink mixed with CQDs using Screen-printing Machine (Grafica Flextronica nano-print 1015, India), and left to dry at 70 °C for 1 h. The reference electrode was sliver paste. An insulating hydrophobic paint was used as a barrier to hold the buffer over the three-electrode sensing area. The aptamer against CPF solutions of 5 μl was drop casted on working electrode and they were kept for the time gap of 4hrs for the correct immobilization of the aptamer in humid chamber. To remove any remaining unbound aptamer, the CQDs/SPEs aptamer electrode was subsequently rinsed with PBS (pH 7.4) and kept at 4 °C for further analysis.

Potato extract was used as real as well as spiked samples. Potato was bought from the nearby market, washed and 100 g of potato was crushed in a mixer followed by centrifugation for 15 min at 10000 rpm at RT, its supernatant was gathered from centrifuge tube and stored in refrigerator for further analysis. The potato extract was spiked with various CPF concentrations during the measurement. When first time potato samples were added without spike, a change in current observed, therefore it was added at different volumes for real sample study.

CQDs were characterized using FTIR to study the functional groups and chemical bonding in 400–3000 cm−1 spectra range. The FTIR spectrum is shown in Fig. 1 (a). The peaks of CQDs at 2851 and 2923 cm−1 were due to C–H bond. The peaks at 1567, 1614, and 1708 cm−1 were assigned to vibrations of NH, amide (CO–NH), and C=O, respectively. Due to the carboxyl groups present on the CQDs, broad peaks from 1337 to 1403 cm−1 appeared, while C–N peak observed at 1221 cm−1 (Jiang et al., 2022). As given in Fig. 1 (b) the zeta potential measurement was conducted to find the overall charge on the CQDs. CQDs have a positive charge of 64.78 ± 7.18 mV. As, PEI acts as a polycationic polymer, it helps in synthesis of positively charged CQDs due to the presences of –NH2 or –NH functional groups (Jiang et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2015). TEM was carried out to study the morphology and size of CQDs. Fig. 1 (c) revealed the spherical shape of CQDs and the size distribution plot in Fig. 1 (d) shows the CQDs average size lies between 2 and 3 nm.

The electrochemical properties of bare and aptamer immobilised CQDs/SPE electrodes were studied using CV and chronoamperometry methods. For this, a 40 μL of PBS (pH 7.4) comprising ferri/ferrocyanide [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was dropped on the three electrodes part of the SPE. The same buffer was used for all the electrochemical studies. The CV curves show a significant variation in peak current with a shift in peak voltage after aptamer immobilization as given in Fig. 2 (A) indicating the successful attachment of the aptamer on the electrode surface. The same trend was observed with the chronoamperometry studies also as shown in Fig. 2 (B). In Fig. 2 (C), (a) the FTIR spectrum of CQDs was given and the peak positions were explained in section 3.1. In Fig. 2 (C), (b) of aptamer, the peaks corresponding to different groups in aptamer were obtained as follows: the peaks appeared at 611 and 1737 cm−1 corresponds to C=O vibrations. The C–N stretching vibrations were obtained at 1090, 1254 cm−1. The peak at 1430 cm−1 was of COO− groups and the peak at 1611 cm−1 represents vibrations of amide (CO–NH). The peak at 1380 cm−1 represents SO2 stretch vibration confirming the thiol functionalization. Few individual functional groups of CQDs and aptamer were disappeared in the CQDs + aptamer FTIR spectrum such as 1567 cm−1 of CQDs and 1737, 1090 and 850 cm−1 of aptamer due to the interaction between aptamer and CQDs. Also, an additional N=N anti-symmetric stretch was found in aptamer + CQDs spectrum indication the interaction between them.

To study the interaction kinetics of CQDs/SPE electrodes, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used at various scan rates ranging 10 - 100 mVs−1. The anodic (Ia) and cathodic (Ic) peak currents increase with scan rate. This increase in peak current was found directly proportional to the under root of scan rate (ν 1/2) which is shown in Fig. 3 with a regression coefficient of 0.99 shows a good linear behaviour confirming that electrochemical reaction was diffusion-controlled process. The diffusion-controlled reactions are the process in which the rate of reaction is equal to the transport rate of the reactants through the buffer solution. It can be concluded from the linear proportionality of peak current values and root of scan rate (Fig. 3). Using Randles–Sevcik equation, (Lakshmi et al., 2016) the diffusion coefficient (D) was estimated from the relation as follows:
I p
=
(
2.69 ×
10 5
)
C
n
3 / 2
D
1 / 2
ν
1 / 2
A
where IP = electrode peak current, n = no. of electrons transferred, A = working electrode surface area (cm2), D = diffusion coefficient (cm2 s−1), C = electrolyte concentration (5 mM), and ν = scan rate (mV s− 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical response study and interference study of aptasensor CQDs/SPE electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Chronoamperometry was used to conduct a response study on aptamer immobilised CQDs/SPE electrodes against CPF. A 10 μL of CPF solution at various concentrations ranges from 1 pM to 500 nM was poured into 30 μL buffer solution and the chronoamperometry was performed for each concentration. The current value after 150s was found to decrease with increasing CPF concentration as shown in Fig. 4 (a) with linear regression coefficient value (R2) of 0.97 (inset). Peak currents were almost the same at higher concentrations, confirming electrode saturation in terms of accessible aptamer binding sites. The calibration plots of current versus CPF concentrations were shown in Fig. 4 (a) inset. The sensitivity of the developed aptasensor was estimated using the formula as sensitivity = slope/surface area (0.1256 cm2). The sensitivity of fabricated apatasensor is found to be 21.39 μA pM−1 cm− 2 with LOD of 0.834 pM using the formula 3σ/m where σ stands for standard deviation of the blank SPE electrode and m stands for slope of calibration plot of Fig. 4 (a) inset (Verma et al., 2021; Yadav et al., 2020). Fig. 4. (b) shows the interference studies and specificity of aptasensor against various interferants such as CPF, Carbofuran, Methyl parathion (MP) Aldicarb (ALD) Monocrotophos (MC) Propoxur and Glucose (Glu), Ca, Mn, Mg, K, Na, Zn ions. This study was performed on the aptasensor electrodes using chronoamperometric technique. During the selectivity test, 5 μL of each interferants (different pesticides and ions) along with 5 μL of CPF were poured into electrolyte solution and observed the response with chronoamperometric technique. Results obtained ascribed the high selectivity for aptamer electrode against various interferants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, stability, spiked studies of potato extract samples against aptasensing
ce:section.ce:para: Reproducibility is the vital parameter for the practical utilization of aptasensors. For examining the CQDs/SPE aptasensor electrodes reproducibility study, six electrodes were fabricated at similar experimental conditions and 1 pM of CPF was added on each electrode and the current was measured and plotted in Fig. 5 (a). The current was almost same and obtained a low value of RSD of 0.34% showing better reproducibility.

The stability of the aptasensor is crucial for determining the potential measurement drifts brought by the effects of aging. The aptasensor electrodes needed for this experiment were made on the same day and stored for further use at 4 °C in the refrigerator. The current was measured for five months, on different days and the current was found to be same as shown in Fig. 5(b). Demonstrating the stability of the aptasensor.

The response study of CPF was examined in spiked samples of potato with various concentrations ranges from 1 pM to 500 nM on CQDs/SPE aptasensor electrodes. Fresh potato extract was used to spiked the CPF concentrations, and measurements were taken under similar circumstances Fig. 5(c). In comparison to CPF analyte sensing, the detection trend in the potato spiked samples is the same. These outcomes approve the pertinency of CQDs/SPE electrochemical sensors in real life.

The potato extract was added directly to the aptasensor along with buffer solution at different volumes from 10 μl to 40 μl and found that the current decreased sequentially up to a volume of 25 μl and saturated after this as shown in Fig. 5(d). It is indicating that the potato extract itself has CPF. The concentration at different volumes was estimated from the calibration plot and given in Table 1
. The LCMS studies were carried out to know the concentration of the CPF in potato extract, but could not get the characteristic peaks as the concentration was too small (less than 50 nM). From Table 1, the concentration of CPF in potato extract is less than 40 nM, which is too small to detect using LCMS. Therefore, the present aptasensor is more sensitive towards the detection of CPF at lower concentrations also (see Table 2).

The description about the fabrication of aptasensor using CQDs onto SPE platform and immobilization of aptamer with various concentration of CPF analyte is shown in Fig. 6 . Positively charge CQDs have been synthesized using citric acid and PEI through microwave-assisted method which further mixed in SPE carbon ink during printing. The charge appeared at CQDs have been confirmed by Zeta Potential as found to be at + 64.78 ± 7.18 mV as shown in Fig. 1. (b).

The aptamer used was custom made specifically for CPF and was thiol end group functionalized, which have the negative charge. The positively charged of CQDs present on the SPE surface can attract the thiol groups because of the presence of –NH2 and –NH functional group. Therefore, the electrostatic interaction take place between positive CQDs and thiol functionalized aptamer. Aptamer form complex with cationic CQDs can lead to the change of aptamer conformation. Thus, more aptamer functionalized on SPE surface can produce greater reduction current. Based on CQDs as electroactive constituent and the aptamer as the recognition receptor form electrostatic interactions that contribute to effective sensing of chlorpyrifos with good specificity and higher sensitivity. The absence of individual peaks of CQDs and aptamer in FTIR spectra further confirmed the interaction between them as explained earlier.

The proposed research work elucidated the development of aptasensor to detect CPF pesticide. The CQDs/SPE electrodes were developed for the study of chlorpyrifos in the linear range of detection from 1 pM (0.445 ng/ml) to 500 nM (0.22 μg/ml). The fabricated aptasensor showed outstanding performance with the LOD of 0.834 pM (0.37 ng/ml). Furthermore, the proposed aptasensor CQDs/SPE electrodes electrochemical sensor show specificity towards interferents and spiked studies with immense sensitivity and reliability. Thus, the present work efficiently showed several advantages such as ease of fabrication, eco-friendly, cost effectiveness and robustness for the detection of chlorpyrifos pesticide.

The project was visualized by GBVSL and PRS. The research problem was conceptualized by GBVSL and PRS. Data curation, formal analysis and experiments were carried out by GBVSL, JKH, AKV and AA. Writing was done by GBVSL and JKH. Supervision and validation were done by PRS. Funding acquisition was done by GBVSL and project administration was done by PRS and GBVSL.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Advanced Instrument Research Facility (AIRF), Jawaharlal Nehru University (JNU), New Delhi, for providing a TEM characterization facility. GBVSL is thankful to DST for funding through DST Women Scientist Project. The authors are also grateful to the Indian Council of medical research (ICMR), India (PAC–SCNS–PS-ICMR-1293) for providing financial support and motivation.
